5 min read
Just now

--

The UN just held the world’s first global AI governance dialogue. The science says the stakes are existential. The architecture says nobody has to do anything
This article was first published on Substack.
On July 6, 2026, representatives of all 193 United Nations member states sat down together for the first time to discuss how humanity should govern artificial intelligence.
The meeting was historic.
It was also revealing.
Two days earlier, the Independent International Scientific Panel on AI — forty experts co-chaired by Turing Award winner Yoshua Bengio and Nobel Peace Prize laureate Maria Ressa — warned that AI capabilities are advancing faster than governments can understand or regulate them, and that no technical guarantee currently exists that the most advanced systems will reliably follow human instructions.
The day before the Dialogue opened, the United States publicly opposed multilateral AI governance at the UN Security Council.
Those three events explain almost everything.
The science says the challenge is becoming existential.
The diplomacy says the world finally has a forum.
The architecture says the forum cannot require anyone to do anything.
